title: Tight Contraction Rates for Primitive Channels under Quantum $f$-Divergences
authors: Matthew Simon Tan, Marco Tomamichel, Ian George
abstract: Data-processing inequalities capture the phenomenon that two probability distributions can only become less distinguishable under any common post-processing. For more fine-grained inequalities, one turns to strong data-processing inequality (SDPI) constants, which give the strongest inequalities for a given channel and reference state for a fixed measure of distinguishability. These quantities have been used to quantify the rate at which time-homogeneous Markov chains contract towards a fixed point both in the classical and quantum setting. In this work, we establish that quantum $f$-divergences satisfy a local reverse Pinsker inequality, which implies the asymptotic contraction rate of a primitive channel to its stationary state is upper bounded by the SDPI constant of any non-commutative $χ^2$-divergence. Using quantum-detailed balance, we establish a sufficient condition for these bounds to be tight. Finally, we apply these results to Petz, Matsumoto, and Hirche-Tomamichel $f$-divergences, establishing new and strengthening previously known results.
summary: This paper establishes new, tight upper bounds on the rate at which quantum channels contract different measures of distinguishability toward a stationary state. By relating f-divergences to chi^2-divergences, the authors provide a framework to calculate exact contraction rates for specific families of quantum divergences. This advances the understanding of information loss in non-commutative quantum processes.
main_problem: Determining the tightest possible asymptotic contraction rates for primitive quantum channels under various quantum f-divergences and establishing conditions for the tightness of these bounds in a non-commutative setting.
main_result: The authors prove that the asymptotic contraction rate of a primitive channel to its stationary state is upper bounded by the SDPI constant of any non-commutative chi^2_g-divergence, and establish that this bound is tight under quantum detailed balance conditions.
method: The study employs Taylor expansions of f-divergences near the identity to establish a local reverse Pinsker inequality, utilizes spectral analysis of channel eigenvalues, and applies the concept of quantum f-detailed balance.
model_or_system: The mathematical model consists of primitive, time-homogeneous quantum channels acting on density matrices in a finite-dimensional Hilbert space, assuming a unique, full-rank stationary state.
key_observables: Strong Data-Processing Inequality (SDPI) constants, quantum f-divergences, and the asymptotic contraction rate of the channel.
important_parameters: The asymptotic regime (n approaching infinity), the primitivity of the channel, and the smoothness (thrice continuous differentiability) of the function f.
main_assumptions: The quantum channels must be primitive, the stationary state must be full-rank, and the function f must be thrice continuously differentiable in a neighborhood of 1.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing a local reverse Pinsker inequality, moves to proving general upper bounds for asymptotic contraction rates, provides conditions for the tightness of these bounds using detailed balance, and concludes with specific applications to Petz, Matsumoto, and Hirche-Tomamichel f-divergences.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems as it provides fundamental bounds on how quickly quantum information/distinguishability decays under the influence of noise and environmental interaction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
